_comment: Contemporary musical setting of selected mantras from the Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad — the shortest of the 10 principal Upaniṣads (only 12 mantras) and the foundational text for the Advaita Vedānta teaching on OM and the four states of consciousness (jāgrat / svapna / suṣupti / turīya). This arrangement includes mantras 1, 2, 6, 7, and 12, with selected phrases extracted and reordered for musical flow, plus a creative summary refrain about the four states. The English meditative interludes are NOT included as lyrics_lines — they're modern overlay, not Sanskrit to learn. Timings are ESTIMATES; adjust after watching the video.
title: Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad (contemporary setting on OM and Turīya)

lines.devanagari: ॐ इत्येतदक्षरम् इदं सर्वं, भूतं भवद् भविष्यद् इति सर्वम् ओङ्कार एव ॥
lines.iast: oṃ ity etad akṣaram idaṃ sarvam, bhūtaṃ bhavad bhaviṣyad iti sarvam oṃkāra eva ||
lines.english_literal: OM — this imperishable syllable is all this; what has been, what is, what shall be — all of it is OM itself.
lines.english_poetic: OM — this one imperishable syllable is all of this. What was, what is, and what shall be — all of it is OM alone.
lines.explanation: Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad mantra 1 (slightly abridged — the canonical text inserts 'tasyopavyākhyānam' meaning 'its explanation follows'). The opening axiom of the entire Upaniṣad: the syllable OM encompasses the whole of time. 'Akṣara' literally means 'imperishable' (a-kṣara = non-decaying) — applied here to OM, the single syllable that does not pass away even when all phenomenal things do.

lines.devanagari: सर्वं ह्येतद् ब्रह्म । अयम् आत्मा ब्रह्म । सोऽयम् आत्मा चतुष्पात् ॥
lines.iast: sarvaṃ hy etad brahma | ayam ātmā brahma | so'yam ātmā catuṣpāt ||
lines.english_literal: All this indeed is Brahman. This Self is Brahman. This very Self is four-quartered.
lines.english_poetic: All this is truly Brahman. This very Self is Brahman. And this Self has four quarters (four states of consciousness).
lines.explanation: Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad mantra 2 — three of the most condensed equations in all of Vedānta. Each is a mahāvākya-class statement. 'Sarvaṃ brahma' (all is Brahman) — the cosmic identity. 'Ayam ātmā brahma' (this Self is Brahman) — the personal identity; this is one of the four great Vedantic mahāvākyas. 'Catuṣpāt' (four-quartered, four-footed) introduces the framework the rest of the Upaniṣad expounds: the four states of consciousness.

lines.english_poetic: Waking — dreaming — deep sleep — and Turīya, beyond even the night.
lines.explanation: A summary refrain naming the four states of consciousness the Upaniṣad analyses one by one in mantras 3-7. Jāgrat is the outward-knowing waking state; svapna is the inward-knowing dream state where the mind generates its own objects; suṣupti is the dreamless deep-sleep state where neither subject nor object appear; turīya ('the fourth') is the ground of all three — pure awareness, present even in dreamless sleep as the witness who 'knows' that nothing was known.

lines.devanagari: ॐ तुरीयं शान्तं शिवम् अद्वैतं प्रपञ्चोपशमं — विभाति ॥
lines.iast: oṃ turīyaṃ śāntaṃ śivam advaitaṃ prapañcopaśamaṃ — vibhāti ||
lines.english_literal: OM — the fourth: peaceful, auspicious, non-dual, the cessation of phenomena — shines forth.
lines.english_poetic: OM. The fourth — peaceful, blissful, non-dual, where the world dissolves — shines.
lines.explanation: Selected phrases from Māṇḍūkya mantra 7's famous catalogue of what turīya IS (the via positiva, following a long via negativa of what it is NOT). 'Śāntam' (peaceful), 'śivam' (auspicious/blissful), 'advaitam' (non-dual) — three of the most celebrated adjectives in all of Vedānta. 'Prapañcopaśama' literally 'the cessation/calming of the phenomenal-spreading-out' — when turīya is recognised, the apparent multiplicity of the world subsides.

words.meaning: the cessation of the phenomenal world, where multiplicity subsides

lines.devanagari: नान्तःप्रज्ञं न बहिष्प्रज्ञं, एकात्मप्रत्ययसारं, अग्राह्यम् अचिन्त्यम् ॥
lines.iast: nāntaḥ-prajñaṃ na bahiṣ-prajñaṃ, ekātma-pratyaya-sāraṃ, agrāhyam acintyam ||
lines.english_literal: Not inward-knowing, not outward-knowing — essence of the one-self-cognition — ungraspable, unthinkable.
lines.english_poetic: Not inward-knowing, not outward-knowing — pure essence of self-awareness — beyond all grasp, beyond all thought.
lines.explanation: From Māṇḍūkya mantra 7's via negativa — selected from a longer catalogue of seven negations (na antaḥ-prajñam, na bahiṣ-prajñam, na ubhayataḥ-prajñam, na prajñāna-ghanam, na prajñam, na aprajñam, etc.). The teaching: turīya is not a kind of knowing turned outward, not a knowing turned inward, not both, not even a thick mass of knowing — it is the ground in which knowing arises. 'Ekātma-pratyaya-sāra' is the most positive phrase in this section: 'the essence (sāra) of the awareness (pratyaya) of the one Self (ekātma)'.

words.meaning: inward-knowing, conscious of inner objects (i.e. dreaming)
outward-knowing, conscious of outer objects (i.e. waking)
the essence of the awareness of the one Self

lines.devanagari: एष सर्वेश्वरः, एषोऽन्तर्यामी, योनिः सर्वस्य, प्रभवाप्ययौ हि भूतानाम् ॥
lines.iast: eṣa sarveśvaraḥ, eṣo'ntaryāmī, yoniḥ sarvasya, prabhavāpyayau hi bhūtānām ||
lines.english_literal: This is the Lord of all, this is the inner controller, the womb/source of all — for he is the origin and the dissolution of beings.
lines.english_poetic: This is the Lord of All, the Inner Controller, the source from which everything springs — origin and end of all beings.
lines.explanation: Māṇḍūkya mantra 6 (the canonical text also includes 'eṣa sarvajñaḥ' / 'this is the all-knowing one'). Three classical Vedantic titles in one breath: sarveśvara (lord of all), antaryāmin (the inner controller — also a key term in the Bṛhadāraṇyaka), yoni (the womb / source). 'Prabhavāpyayau' is a single compound for 'arising and dissolution' — the two endpoints of every being's life, both inside Brahman.

words.meaning: of beings, of all that has come into being

lines.english_literal: Mind is peaceful — the phenomenal world is empty — the fourth.
lines.english_poetic: When mind is at peace and the world is empty — Turīya roars as silent light.
lines.explanation: A contemporary composition (not from the Upaniṣad text) using Vedantic vocabulary to describe the experiential approach to turīya. The phrasing echoes the Yoga Sūtras (cittaṃ śāntaṃ recalls 'yogaś citta-vṛtti-nirodhaḥ') joined to the Madhyamaka-influenced Vedantic notion of phenomenal emptiness. The songwriter's own meditation on the canonical material that precedes and follows.

words.meaning: the phenomenal world, the expanding multiplicity

lines.devanagari: ॐ तुरीयं शान्तं शिवम् अद्वैतं, कैवल्यं परमं पदम् ॥
lines.iast: oṃ turīyaṃ śāntaṃ śivam advaitaṃ, kaivalyaṃ paramaṃ padam ||
lines.english_literal: OM. The fourth, peaceful, auspicious, non-dual — absolute solitude, the highest state.
lines.english_poetic: OM. The fourth — peace, bliss, non-duality — liberation, the highest state. Ascend.
lines.explanation: Composite: 'turīyaṃ śāntaṃ śivam advaitam' is from Māṇḍūkya 7; 'kaivalyaṃ paramaṃ padam' is a phrase from the wider Yoga-Vedānta vocabulary (kaivalya is the goal-term of Patañjali's Yoga Sūtras — total isolation of pure consciousness from phenomenal entanglement). The songwriter joins the Vedāntic and Yogic terms to point to the same realisation.

lines.english_literal: OM without measure — silence becomes sound.
lines.english_poetic: OM beyond all measure — silence itself becomes sound.
lines.explanation: Closing line evoking Māṇḍūkya mantra 12: 'amātraś caturtho 'vyavahāryaḥ prapañcopaśamaḥ śivo'dvaita evam oṃkāra ātmaiva' — 'The measureless (a-mātra) fourth is the untransactable, the cessation of phenomena, blissful, non-dual; thus OM itself is the Self.' OM is decomposed into its three measurable sounds (a-u-m) corresponding to the three measurable states; the fourth 'measure' is the silence after the m — and that silence is turīya. The song closes by becoming that silence.
